title: Patchouli and sandalwood soy jar candle
subtitle: A 200 ml container candle with two earthy, grounding base notes
excerpt: Patchouli and sandalwood are both low-volatility base notes that release slowly in a soy container candle, giving a steady, earthy, woody scent from the first burn to the last. The two oils are blended at 4.6% patchouli and 3.4% sandalwood against the wax weight. A CD-12 wick in a 200 ml jar gives a 25 to 30-hour burn.
sourceNotes: Soy wax container candle technique: Candlescience wick sizing guide (2023); CD-12 for 6 to 7 cm jar diameter. Fragrance load at 8% by wax weight: maximum for GW 464 soy wax. Patchouli EO (Pogostemon cablin): dark, earthy, persistent base note; darkens in colour over time, which is normal. Sandalwood EO: use sustainably sourced Santalum album (Indian sandalwood) or Santalum spicatum (Australian sandalwood); Amyris balsamifera is a more affordable alternative with a similar creamy-woody character.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 200 ml jar candle (130 g soy wax fill)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale weights by the number of jars. One pre-tabbed wick per jar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure for 3 to 5 days before burning. Base-note-heavy blends often improve after several days of curing.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Wax stable for 5 years or more. Patchouli and sandalwood are base notes with excellent longevity; fragrance should remain strong for 2 to 3 years. Keep the lid on between burns.

glossaryTerms.definition: The scent given off by a candle while it is burning. Base notes like patchouli and sandalwood perform well on the hot throw because their heavy molecules volatilise slowly and consistently through the burn.
The temperature at which liquid wax is poured into the container. Soy container wax is poured at 55 to 65°C; too hot and the wax pulls away from the sides; too cool and it sets unevenly.

content.text: Patchouli and sandalwood are both base notes: low-volatility, slow-releasing, earthy, and woody. Where most fragrance blends use base notes to anchor brighter top or mid notes, this candle puts them front and centre. The result is a deep, grounding scent with good

throughout the burn. Patchouli darkens in colour over time, which can give the wax a slight amber tint; this is normal and harmless. Sandalwood essential oil is expensive; Amyris balsamifera (sometimes sold as West Indian sandalwood) gives a similar creamy-woody character at a lower cost and is not an endangered species.

items.prepNote: Container-blend soy wax; GW 464 or equivalent
4.6% of wax weight; dark, earthy base note
3.4% of wax weight; creamy, woody base note; sustainably sourced
CD-12 for a 6 to 7 cm diameter jar

content.text: Place the soy wax in the candle pouring pitcher. Set in a pan of barely simmering water and heat, stirring occasionally, until the wax is fully melted and reaches 80°C. Remove from the heat and allow to cool.

Secure the sustainer tab of the pre-tabbed wick to the centre of the jar base with melted wax or a wick sticker. Thread the wick through the centring bar and lay the bar across the jar rim so the wick hangs straight in the centre.
Add the essential oils and pour
When the wax has cooled to 60°C, add the patchouli and sandalwood essential oils. Stir for 2 minutes. Pour at
: between 55 and 65°C. Pour down the side of the jar in a slow, steady stream, leaving a 5 to 10 mm gap at the top. Leave undisturbed until fully set.
Cure for at least 1 day before burning; base-note-heavy blends often benefit from 3 to 5 days of curing. Trim the wick to 5 mm before each burn.

Keep the lid on between burns. The wax is stable for 5 years or more; patchouli and sandalwood are base notes with excellent longevity, so the fragrance should remain strong for 2 to 3 years. Patchouli may give the wax a slight amber tint over time; this is cosmetic only.
